home.title: Solidis
home.subtitle: The fastest Redis client for Node.js. Zero dependencies, 2x+ faster than ioredis, battle-tested in production.
home.whyDescription: The reasons why teams in production choose Solidis over alternatives.
home.serverlessReadyDesc: Under 29 KB min bundle with zero dependencies. Critical for cold start performance in serverless environments like AWS Lambda and Vercel Edge.
home.twiceAsFastDesc: Up to 2.1x faster across 15 benchmark categories with zero-copy RESP parsing, automatic pipeline batching, and setImmediate coalescing.
home.battleTestedDesc: 98%+ test coverage with 2+ years in production and zero error reports. Built to run without surprises.
home.modernProtocolDesc: All 17 RESP3 data types including Map, Set, Push, BigNumber. Tree-shakable architecture — import only the commands you need.
home.highPerformanceDesc: Optimized for speed with automatic pipeline batching and zero-copy buffer operations
home.solidArchitectureDesc: Clean, maintainable code following SOLID principles for better extensibility
home.typescriptFirstDesc: Full TypeScript support with comprehensive type definitions and IntelliSense
home.blazingFastDesc: Solidis delivers up to 2x faster performance than ioredis with zero dependencies
home.getStartedDesc: Install Solidis and start building high-performance Redis applications with just a few lines of code.
home.communityDesc: Join our growing community of developers using Solidis in production
home.comprehensiveDocsDesc: Everything you need to master Solidis
home.gettingStartedCard: Step-by-step guides for beginners and experienced developers
home.apiReferenceCard: Complete API documentation with examples and type definitions
home.tutorialsCard: Learn by building real-world applications and use cases
home.readyToStartDesc: Install Solidis and start building high-performance Redis applications today.
home.benchmarkDesc: Up to 2.1x faster than ioredis across 15 benchmark categories. 100K iterations, 10K concurrency, 1 KB payload.
home.zeroDepsDesc: No transitive dependencies. Your supply chain stays clean.
home.fullRespDesc: All 17 RESP3 data types including Map, Set, Push, BigNumber.
home.typescriptStrictDesc: Per-command I/O types with runtime reply guards.
home.pipelineBatchingDesc: setImmediate coalescing with configurable yield points.
home.autoReconnectDesc: Configurable backoff with auto-recovery for subscriptions.
home.treeShakableDesc: Import only the commands you need. Under 29 KB min bundle.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Find quick answers to common questions about Solidis. Can't find what you're looking for?
faq.stillNeedHelpDesc: If you can't find the answer you're looking for, here are some ways to get help
faq.githubDiscussionsDesc: Ask questions and get community help
faq.q1: What is Solidis and how is it different from other Redis clients?
faq.a1: Solidis is a high-performance RESP client built with SOLID principles in mind. It offers better type safety and follows clean architecture patterns compared to traditional Redis clients. It's designed for modern TypeScript applications with a focus on maintainability and performance.
faq.a2: Solidis supports any RESP-compatible server, including Redis 5.0 and above, as well as cloud Redis services like AWS ElastiCache and Azure Cache for Redis.
faq.q3: Can I use Solidis in production?
faq.a3: Yes! Solidis is production-ready and includes features like automatic reconnection with configurable retry attempts and delays, comprehensive error handling, and pipeline optimization to ensure reliability in production deployments.
faq.q4: How does Solidis handle command pipelining?
faq.a4: Solidis automatically batches commands into pipelines to reduce network round trips. You can configure the maximum number of commands per pipeline via the maxCommandsPerPipeline option (default: 300). This significantly improves throughput when executing multiple Redis operations.
faq.q5: What's the performance compared to other Redis clients?
faq.a5: Solidis is optimized for high throughput and low latency. Benchmarks show it performs up to 2x faster than ioredis across 15 different operation types (100K iterations, 10K concurrency), with the added benefits of better TypeScript support and cleaner architecture.
faq.a6: Yes, Solidis supports Redis transactions via the MULTI/EXEC pattern. Use client.multi() to start a transaction, queue commands, then call exec() to execute them atomically.
faq.q7: How do I configure connection timeouts?
faq.a7: You can configure both connection and command timeouts when creating a SolidisClient instance. Use the connectionTimeout option (default: 2000ms) for connection establishment timeout and commandTimeout (default: 5000ms) for individual command execution timeout.
faq.a8: Set the authentication option in your SolidisClient configuration with username and password fields. For Redis 6+ with ACL users, you can specify both the username and password.
faq.a9: Solidis supports both RESP2 (default) and RESP3 protocols. You can configure the protocol version via the protocol option when creating a SolidisClient instance.
faq.q10: How do I handle connection errors?
faq.a10: Solidis provides comprehensive error handling with specific error types like SolidisConnectionError, SolidisClientError, and SolidisRequesterError. The client supports automatic reconnection with configurable retry attempts (maxConnectionRetries, default: 20) and delays (connectionRetryDelay, default: 100ms).
faq.q11: Why am I getting timeout errors?
faq.a11: Timeout errors can occur due to network issues, Redis server overload, or misconfigured timeout values. Check your connectionTimeout (default: 2000ms) and commandTimeout (default: 5000ms) settings, and ensure your Redis server is responsive.
faq.q12: How do I debug connection issues?
faq.a12: Enable debug logging by setting debug: true in your SolidisClient configuration. Listen for debug events via client.on('debug', (entry) => console.log(entry)) to get detailed logging for connection events, command execution, and errors.

architecture.title: Architecture Overview
architecture.subtitle: Understanding Solidis's internal structure and SOLID design principles.
architecture.coreDesc: Core entry point that creates and coordinates all components
architecture.connReconnect: Automatic reconnection with configurable retry delay
architecture.connRecovery: Auto-recovery for database selection and subscriptions
architecture.requesterDesc: Handles command pipelining and request states
architecture.parserDesc: Processes RESP2/RESP3 protocol with optimized buffer handling
architecture.pubsubDesc: Maintains subscription state for pub/sub functionality
architecture.srpDesc: Each component has a single, well-defined responsibility
architecture.srpExample: Parser only handles RESP protocol parsing, not connection management
architecture.ocpDesc: Open for extension, closed for modification via the extension system
architecture.ocpExample: Add custom commands without modifying core client code
architecture.lspDesc: Components can be replaced with alternative implementations
architecture.lspExample: Different parser implementations for RESP2 vs RESP3
architecture.dipDesc: Components depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations
architecture.dipExample: Client depends on parser interface, not specific parser implementation
architecture.flow1Desc: User calls a command method (e.g., client.set('key', 'value'))
architecture.flow2Desc: Command is added to the pipeline queue for batch processing
architecture.flow3Desc: Command is serialized to RESP protocol and sent through the TCP socket
architecture.flow4Desc: Parser reads and parses RESP response from Redis using zero-copy operations
architecture.flow5Desc: Parsed response is matched to the original command and the promise is resolved
architecture.flow6Desc: The parsed result is returned to the user's await statement
architecture.systemArchitectureDesc: Overview of how Solidis components work together
architecture.solidDesc: Solidis is built following SOLID design principles, ensuring maintainability, testability, and extensibility.
architecture.commandFlowDesc: How commands flow through the Solidis architecture
architecture.perfDesc: Key architectural decisions that make Solidis fast
architecture.zeroCopyDesc: The parser uses buffer slicing instead of copying data, reducing memory allocations and improving throughput for large payloads.
architecture.pipelineBatchingDesc: Commands are automatically batched in pipelines to reduce network round trips, configurable via maxCommandsPerPipeline.
architecture.bufferMgmtDesc: Smart buffer allocation and reuse strategies minimize garbage collection pressure and memory overhead.
architecture.lazyConnDesc: Optional lazy connection mode delays connection establishment until needed, reducing resource usage for idle clients.

apiReference.title: API Reference
apiReference.subtitle: Complete reference documentation for all Solidis classes, methods, and interfaces.
apiReference.mainClassDesc: The main client class for interacting with Redis servers
apiReference.connectDesc: Establishes a connection to the Redis server. Must be called before performing any operations.
apiReference.disconnectDesc: Closes the connection to the Redis server and cleans up resources.
apiReference.setDesc: Sets a key to hold the string value.
apiReference.getDesc: Gets the value of a key.
apiReference.transactionsDesc: Redis transactions allow the execution of a group of commands in a single step, using the MULTI/EXEC pattern to perform transactions atomically.
apiReference.pipelinesDesc: Pipelining sends multiple commands without waiting for replies, then reads all replies in a single step. This reduces network round trips and improves throughput.
apiReference.pubsubDesc: Redis Pub/Sub implements messaging where publishers send messages to channels, and subscribers who subscribe to those channels receive them.
apiReference.customCommandsDesc: Extend Solidis with custom commands to create higher-level abstractions. The extension system allows adding new features without modifying core code.
apiReference.rawCommandsDesc: Use the raw send method to execute any Redis command not yet implemented. Sends commands directly through the RESP protocol.
apiReference.debuggingDesc: Solidis provides detailed debugging capabilities to help troubleshoot issues. You can track connection status, command execution, errors, and more.
apiReference.errorBase: Base error class for all Solidis errors
apiReference.errorCommand: Errors during command execution or reply handling
apiReference.eventConnect: Emitted when the client connects to the server
apiReference.eventReady: Emitted when the client is ready to send commands
apiReference.eventClose: Emitted when the connection is closed
apiReference.eventMessage: Emitted when a message is received on a subscribed channel
apiReference.eventShardMessage: Emitted when a message is received on a shard channel
apiReference.eventDebug: Emitted when a debug event is logged

benchmarks.zeroDepsDesc2: No overhead from external libraries, pure Node.js implementation
benchmarks.zeroCopyParserDesc: Buffer slices without copying, intelligent shift thresholds
benchmarks.pipelineCoalescingDesc: Commands batched within the same tick for fewer round trips
benchmarks.chunkedWritesDesc: Backpressure-aware writes with configurable chunk sizes
benchmarks.readySpeedDesc: Install Solidis and see the performance difference yourself.

contributing.title: Contributing to Solidis
contributing.subtitle: Solidis is an open-source project and we welcome contributions from the community. Here's how you can help make Solidis better.
contributing.codeContributionsDesc: Fix bugs, add features, or improve performance
contributing.documentationContDesc: Improve docs, add examples, or fix typos
contributing.testingDesc: Add tests or improve test coverage
contributing.step1Desc: Create a new branch for your feature or bug fix
contributing.step2Desc: Follow the code style guidelines and write tests for your changes
contributing.step3Desc: Ensure all tests pass and add new tests if needed
contributing.step5Desc: Push your branch and create a pull request
contributing.codeQualityDesc: Standards to follow when contributing code
contributing.tsRule1: Use strict typing and avoid any types where possible
contributing.tsRule2: Avoid type casting with as unless absolutely necessary
contributing.tsRule3: Provide comprehensive type definitions for all public APIs
contributing.perfRule1: Consider performance implications of your changes
contributing.perfRule3: Use benchmarks to validate performance improvements
contributing.depsRule1: Avoid adding new dependencies unless absolutely necessary
contributing.depsRule3: Justify any new dependencies in your PR description
contributing.testRule1: Write tests for all new features and bug fixes
contributing.testRule2: Ensure existing tests continue to pass
contributing.testRule3: Include edge cases and error scenarios in tests
contributing.prGuidelinesDesc: What to include in your pull request
contributing.joinCommunityDesc: Connect with other contributors and get help
